meta.panel_summary: The weakest link is a confirmed T0 arithmetic error: the reported 31.1% increase does not match the recomputed 45.17% for $3,004->$4,361, a fail that reproduced deterministically and aligns with the journal's own correction to 45.2%. Beyond the arithmetic, the panel notes the claim's 'exact' strength is overstated for a corrected figure and its interpretation is sensitive to nominal-vs-real dollars and a pandemic-year endpoint, and should not be read as causal or population-aggregate. Integrity-wise the error appears benign, conservative in direction, and was properly corrected, so no human escalation on integrity grounds.

findings.message: reported percent change does not match the recomputed old->new change

evidence.quote: total medical spending rose from $3004 (2017) to $4361 (2021), a 31.1% increase

location.quote: total medical spending rose from $3004 (2017) to $4361 (2021), a 31.1% increase

routed_to_human.note: Strength 'exact' miscalibrated for a figure the authors formally corrected; 'Total medical spending' risks reading as a population aggregate when figures are per-household means; single-arm descriptive trend does not support condition-attributable/causal framing (no comparison group).
routed_to_human.quote: Total medical spending rose from $3,004 (2017) to $4,361 (2021), a 31.1% increase

routed_to_human.note: Result hinges on nominal vs. inflation-adjusted dollars (CPI-U ~13-14% over 2017-2021); 'total spending' blends insurer- and patient-paid amounts so it does not equal household burden; 2021 endpoint captures pandemic-era anomalies, and a two-point comparison cannot distinguish a durable trend from a spike.

Low/no-action signal: 31.1% is consistent with a wrong-denominator computation (change divided by new value), error direction is conservative (understates the increase), and a published correction (PMC11022109, 45.2%) was appropriately issued. No escalation warranted.
